metadata.experiment: HELIOS Fleet Study - Experiment 2: Parallelism Overhead Analysis

sweet_spot_analysis.reason: Optimal balance between speedup (2.3x), efficiency (58%), and overhead
sweet_spot_analysis.why_not_8: Context switching overhead exceeds parallelism benefit; speedup DECREASES
sweet_spot_analysis.why_not_16: Catastrophic failure: 3.5x slower than baseline due to feature duplication

key_findings.description: Efficiency drops from 100% (1 agent) to 4% (16 agents), showing overhead grows faster than agent count
Beyond 4 agents, overhead dominates and speedup decreases; 4 agents provides optimal ROI
8-agent configuration is actually 104ms SLOWER than 4-agent, demonstrating context switching penalty
Theory predicts 13.3x speedup at 16 agents; actual is 0.631x. Overhead is superlinear, not constant
16-agent design with all agents building all modules (2×) is 3.5x slower than sequential

recommendations.action: Detect CPU core count; cap at 4 agents
recommendations.rationale: Prevents worst-case overhead on under-resourced systems
recommendations.implementation: Runtime detection, fallback to sequential on single-core

recommendations.action: Use recursive 4-agent parallelism for large workloads
recommendations.rationale: Avoids overhead trap; better scaling for 16+ modules
16-agent with duplication is 3.5x slower than baseline
recommendations.implementation: Design: each agent handles unique subset

recommendations.rationale: Track setup, coordination, context switching separately

visualization_suggestions: Plot 1: Speedup Curve (actual vs ideal vs Amdahl)
Plot 2: Efficiency vs Agent Count (declining curve)
Plot 3: Execution Time vs Agent Count (U-shaped after agent 4)
Plot 5: Amdahl Law Comparison (measured vs predicted)
